text.title: SERP Intent Classification Rubric Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules that force every target query through an evidence-anchored intent classification before drafting. Unanchored intent tags = AI slop downstream.

rule.statement: The primary intent MUST be one of exactly four classes: Informational (I), Comparative (C), Transactional (T), Navigational (N). Custom classes ("educational-transactional") = REJECT. Sub-types are allowed inside each class (e.g. I:how-to / I:definition / C:vs / C:best-list / T:buy / T:tool-signup) but the primary letter is one-of-four.
rule.rationale: Open-ended intent vocabularies become noise; teams disagree on labels. A closed set is the only way to keep the rubric repeatable across taggers.

rule.statement: Every classification MUST cite at least TWO independent SERP signals: (a) page-type distribution in top-10 (blog / comparison / product / docs / video / forum), (b) SERP features present (featured snippet / PAA / shopping / image pack), (c) "related searches" framing, (d) snippet word patterns ("how", "best", "vs", "buy"). Classification without evidence = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Gut-feel intent tags are wrong ~30% of the time on long-tail queries. SERP-evidence anchoring is what makes the classification falsifiable.

rule.statement: A secondary intent MAY be tagged ONLY if ≥ 30% of top-10 results clearly serve a different class than the primary. Otherwise secondary intent stays empty. Defaulting to secondary = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Auto-adding secondaries dilutes the contract for drafters. Secondary intent should only appear when the SERP itself is split.

rule.statement: If a query genuinely serves two distinct intent classes with comparable SERP weight (e.g. half informational, half transactional), the rubric MUST recommend SPLIT — produce two briefs, one per intent — not a hedge "covers both" classification.
rule.rationale: Hedged content serves neither audience. Splitting at classification time is cheaper than rewriting after the article fails to rank.

rule.statement: If SERP top-10 has fewer than 3 dominant page types and no clear SERP features, the rubric MUST tag intent=AMBIGUOUS and block content production pending user research (interviews, GSC query data, paid-search intent data). Forcing a classification on ambiguous SERPs = REJECT.
rule.rationale: Ambiguous SERPs are usually emerging or thin topics. Drafting blindly is gambling; the right move is research, not classification by gut.

rule.statement: Navigational intent MUST distinguish brand-N (a specific company/product) from category-N (a generic site type like "best CRM"). Generic queries tagged Navigational because the SERP shows brand pages are misclassified — they are Comparative or Transactional. Mis-tagging brand-N = REJECT.
rule.rationale: "Best CRM" surfaces brand pages, but the user is comparing, not navigating. Conflating these wastes effort building branded content for non-brand queries.

example.title: Classification output
example.text: query="hubspot vs pipedrive" primary=C:vs secondary=null evidence=[top-10: 7 comparison-blog, 2 brand-pages, 1 review-site | features: featured_snippet=comparison_table, PAA=present, video=absent | related: "hubspot vs salesforce", "pipedrive review"] recommendation: SINGLE_BRIEF drafter_contract: comparison table mandatory, "best for whom" verdicts mandatory
